steps.text: A printout, buried under three others. 47Hz sustained tone. Duration 4:12. Timestamp reads 04:00. In handwriting beneath: 'canal. Third night running.'
A hand-drawn map pinned beneath the printouts. A canal route, annotated with Xs at intervals. One circled twice, in heavier ink. The route drawn doesn't match the canal outside the window.
A photograph pinned below the map. Brick wall, no windows, ivy up the left side. An address written on the back — the street name stops mid-word. The building name is crossed out.
She hums it without pausing her work. Three bars, a held breath, three bars again. The same breath each time. The same place in the melody.

Your notebook, open on the table. The margins are full of notation you don't remember writing. The same melody, in fragments — each transcription slightly different. The most recent one is from today.
A groove worn into the floorboard. Foot-shaped, oriented toward the window. Beside it, a circular burn in the wood — the kind a mic stand makes when it stays in one place for years.

choice.prompt: You know the song's origin. The barista is humming a song written in your flat by a person who died before she was born.

options.text: That song. I know where it comes from.

consequence.narrativeText: She stops. 'What do you mean?' You tell her. She tries to hum it again. She can't. The melody is gone. 'I don't... I can't remember how it goes.' She looks at her hands.
She hums. You listen. The melody fills the cafe the way it has every morning. Some knowledge is better as a secret. The mystery deepens. The music stays.

flatObject.description: Nothing on the shelf. But you know.

steps.text: You count the sides of the shadow. Five. The church has four. You count the shadow again. Five.
You come back at night. The shadow stretches north-west. The moon is in the east. You stand still for a long time. Neither moves.
The board reads: MERIDIAN — 3 MIN. The platform gets cold. The man beside you checks his phone. The board flips: Canary Wharf — 3 min. He looks up. No train came.
The same figure. Different locations. Always still while everyone else moves. You've seen them at the basin. The churchyard. The lot. Watching.
You look directly at the figure. They don't leave. For the first time, they hold your gaze. A stillness that has nothing to do with patience. Then they turn and walk — not away. Through.
